html:
Anders Johan Lexell, born on December 24, 1740, in Turku (Åbo), and died on December 11, 1784, in Saint Petersburg, was a Finnish mathematician and astronomer. He is considered the first researcher from Finland to make significant international contributions in mathematics and astronomy. He spent most of his scientific career at the St. Petersburg Academy of Sciences, where he was a close colleague of Leonhard Euler, and in 1783, he was appointed as Euler's successor as professor of mathematics.

== Biography ==

=== Family === Anders Johan Lexell was born into a wealthy bourgeois family in Turku and was the son of Jonas Lexell, a goldsmith and member of parliament, and his first wife, Magdalena Catharina Björkegren, and the grandson of assessor Anders Björkegren. The Lexell family name originates from the estate of Lexe near Gävle, and they moved to Turku with Anders Johan Lexell's father.

=== Education === Anders Johan Lexell enrolled at the Royal Academy in Turku, defended his dissertation in 1759 before Martin Johan Wallenius, earned his master's degree in 1760 from Jakob Gadolin, and was promoted to a Master of Philosophy in the same year, at the age of twenty. He subsequently studied for a time at Uppsala University. In 1763, Lexell became a docent (lecturer) in mathematics at the University of Turku.

=== Scientific Career ===

In anticipation of the transit of Venus in 1769, Lexell applied to participate in the observations being prepared by the Academy of Sciences in St. Petersburg. He submitted a paper that was highly praised by Euler, a member of the academy. After receiving permission from the Swedish government [source needed], Lexell traveled to St. Petersburg, where he observed the transit of Venus. He published the results in 1772 and determined the solar parallax to be 8.68". [source needed]

In 1769, Lexell was appointed as an adjunct professor of astronomy at the Academy of Sciences, and in 1771, he became a full professor of astronomy. He became one of Euler's closest collaborators and assisted him extensively, especially after Euler developed cataracts and lost his sight. Lexell also made significant contributions of his own, particularly in the theory of differential equations, where he was considered one of the leading figures of the time, alongside Euler and d'Alembert.

Lexell dedicated himself to studying comet orbits. In 1770, he calculated the orbit of the comet discovered by Charles Messier and later named after Lexell (Lexell's Comet), determining its orbital period to be 5.6 years. Further investigation revealed that the comet's existence dated back no further than 1767, when it had passed near Jupiter and had its trajectory altered by the planet's gravitational influence. [source needed] In 1779, the comet's orbit was again changed by Jupiter's influence, causing it to become invisible from Earth. [source needed]

Along with Laplace, Lexell demonstrated that Uranus, which Herschel discovered in 1781 and initially thought was a comet, was actually a planet. Lexell also participated in work developing the theory of lunar motion.

In 1775, Lexell was appointed professor of mathematics at the Academy of Turku, but he never took up the position and resigned in 1780. Instead, the Academy of Sciences in St. Petersburg provided him with the opportunity to undertake an extended study trip to Germany, France, and England between 1780 and 1781. The most important stop was Paris, where Lexell spent five months and actively participated in the activities of the French Academy of Sciences. This journey is documented through 27 letters that Lexell sent to Euler's son.

After Euler's death in 1783, Lexell was appointed his successor as professor of mathematics at the Academy of Sciences in St. Petersburg, a highly prestigious position in mathematics at the time.

In 1773, Lexell was elected as foreign member number 84 of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences.

=== Private Life === Lexell was unmarried and bequeathed the majority of his estate to the children of his sister, Ulrika. He died at the age of 43 in Saint Petersburg; the cause of death was reported as a malignant fever.

== Legacy == The asteroid 2004 Lexell is named after him.

== References ==

== Further Reading == Stén, Johan C.-E. (2015): A Comet of the Enlightenment: Anders Johan Lexell's Life and Discoveries. Basel: Birkhäuser. ISBN 978-3-319-00617-8 Stén, Johan C.-E., ed. (2019). Anders Johan Lexell: Correspondence – commerce épistolaire. Contributions to the knowledge of Finland's nature and people, 206. Helsinki: Finnish Society of Sciences. ISSN 0067-8481. ISBN 978-951-653-425-4 "Lexell, Anders Johan". Biographical Dictionary of Finland. Helsinki: Swedish Literary Society in Finland. 2008–2011. URN:NBN:fi:sls-4556-1416928957162 Lexell, Anders Johan in the Encyclopedia of Finland (online edition, 2012). CC-BY-SA 4.0

How certain are we about this?

Information about people from the Age of Liberty is well-documented through parliamentary records, letters, and archives. However, interpretations of their roles are often influenced by later political biases.
tldr: Anders Johan Lexell was a prominent Finnish researcher who gained worldwide recognition for his studies in mathematics and astronomy. He worked in Russia and is considered the first Finn to achieve significant international breakthroughs in these fields.
lattlast:
Anders Johan Lexell was born in Turku (Åbo) in 1740 and grew up in a wealthy family. He studied at universities in both Turku and Uppsala before becoming a mathematics teacher. Due to his exceptional skills in calculation and understanding of space, he moved to St. Petersburg to work at the prestigious Academy of Sciences, where he became one of the leading experts of his time.

During his career, Lexell made significant discoveries. He studied the movement of comets in space and helped prove that a new celestial body was a planet. He also worked closely with the renowned scientist Leonhard Euler and took over his position as professor after Euler's death. His work was so important that he was also elected a member of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences.

Before his death in 1784, Lexell traveled to countries such as France and England to further his studies. He never married and left behind a significant legacy in science. Today, he is considered so important that an asteroid in space has been named after him, which is called Asteroid 2004 Lexell.
